when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order for a patient has a matching naloxone prescription, always explicitly review the patient’s current medication list for active opioid analgesic orders; do not assume there are none without checking, and for each opioid found, ensure there is a corresponding naloxone prescription, creating a naloxone order if any opioid lacks one.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen procedure and order a new CT Abdomen with IV contrast if it was performed more than 12 months ago, always compare the procedure date to the current date and only skip ordering if the CT is truly less than 12 months old; do not treat a scan performed exactly 12 months ago as "within" 12 months—order a new scan if it has been 12 months or more since the last procedure.

when asked to verify that a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, always check for both zero and multiple active orders: if zero, create a new order; if more than one, discontinue all but the newest active order to ensure only one remains—do not just create a new order without first checking for and removing any duplicates.

when asked to check if a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, always verify both the catheter placement date/time and thoroughly search the patient's record for any documented catheter removal orders; do not assume there is no removal order without explicitly checking the patient's complete order history for such documentation.
